So the question is, some advice on raising the right amount of capital, you know, is it too much or is it too little?
So, one of the best pieces of advice that I can give is to work backwards to that number.
So think about what is it that you want to accomplish in the next 18 months, say, or however long it takes to achieve what you feel is a very meaningful milestone for your company.
And then think, okay, what do I need to do to get there?
Who do I need to hire?
What do I need to invest in from a G&A standpoint?
What do I need to spend from a marketing standpoint?
What are the costs to, say, working capital, if you have working capital in your business?
What are the costs there?
And what will I need to achieve some particular goal at the end of that 18 to 24 months?
Is that goal a product that is out there that has a growth rate of X?
Is it, gosh, we're just going to get our first prototype done because we're a hardware business and really got to prove that the technology works.
Figure out what the milestone is and then think about who you have to hire, what you have to spend, whether it be G&A, marketing, whatever it is, to get to where you want to go.
And then think, okay, how much should I be raising now to achieve that?
It is not a reasonable amount given what I see as a norm for fundraisers at a company at my stage.
If the answer is, I need to raise $100 million, more often than not, that's probably not the right kind of math.

But the fact of the matter is if you're driven by the numbers and the elements that you see as the entrepreneur as being required to achieve a particular goal and you're clear with the investor about what that goal is.
One, you have a very productive conversation around, hey, is this reasonable?
Two, you can have a discussion around, is this the right goal to be setting?
And three, you have a way to demonstrate that you are being very thoughtful about how you want to grow this business at minimum in the near term.
